food_subgroup: Battered deep-fried pork with sweet-sour sauce; prepared Cantonese restaurant dish

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Cantonese restaurant serving of Sweet and Sour Pork is approximately 280 g, comprising battered deep-fried pork pieces with pineapple, bell pepper, and onion coated in sweet-sour ketchup-vinegar-sugar sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet and Sour Pork — Component Calculation (USDA SR Legacy + HPB Singapore cross-check)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) with HPB Singapore cross-check
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from component-level calculation using USDA SR Legacy pork loin, cornstarch batter, ketchup, pineapple, bell pepper, and onion entries, cross-checked against HPB Singapore food composition data for comparable Cantonese pork dishes. No single approved-source entry for this exact composite dish exists; values represent a weighted ingredient-level estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Battered Fried Pork with Sweet-Sour Sauce — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (Sweet-Sour Sauce Dishes and Deep-Fried Battered Pork)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Cantonese Sweet and Sour Pork exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~72 (high) is estimated based on: (1) cornstarch/wheat flour deep-fried batter (GI ~70–80 range for fried starchy coatings), (2) high-sugar ketchup-vinegar-sugar sauce contributing rapidly absorbed sucrose, and (3) analogous sweet-sour sauce dishes in the GI literature. GL ~36 calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 50.1) / 100 ≈ 36.1. Carbohydrate per serving: 17.9 g/100g × 280g serving = ~50.1g. Confidence: 0.85.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~800 mg per 280 g serving, approximately 33–40% of the 2000 mg daily limit) from ketchup, soy sauce marinade, and oyster sauce components
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Sweet and Sour Pork to occasional consumption; request 'sauce on the side' or 'less sauce' to reduce sodium intake by approximately 30%, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~36 per serving) driven by cornstarch/flour deep-fried batter and high-sugar ketchup-vinegar-sugar sauce (~20 g sugar per serving), producing a rapid and large postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Sweet and Sour Pork as a special-occasion food only; if consumed, limit to half a serving, substantially reduce accompanying rice, and consider requesting steamed or stir-fried pork without batter as a lower-GL alternative at the same restaurant.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~4.8 g per serving) from pork and frying oil, combined with high sodium (~800 mg per serving) and high added sugar (~20 g per serving) contributing to cardiometabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption due to the combined burden of sodium, added sugar, and frying-derived fat; choosing steamed or braised pork dishes with minimal sauce is preferred for regular Chinese restaurant meals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High GL (~36) promotes AGE formation and pro-inflammatory cytokine patterns; high sugar content (~20 g per serving) and deep-frying-derived polyunsaturated linoleic acid may contribute to arachidonic acid pool and pro-inflammatory eicosanoid production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Sweet and Sour Pork to monthly consumption at most; Vitamin C (~35 mg per serving from bell pepper and pineapple) provides a partial anti-inflammatory offset, but lower-GL alternatives such as Mapo Tofu or Steamed Fish are preferred for regular Chinese restaurant visits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Pork (Cantonese Style), known in Cantonese as Gu Lou Yuk (咕嚕肉) and in Mandarin as Gūlūròu (咕噜肉), is one of the most iconic and globally recognised Cantonese Chinese dishes. It consists of bite-sized pieces of pork loin or shoulder that are marinated, coated in a cornstarch-wheat flour batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried pork pieces are then stir-fried and coated in a vibrant sweet-sour sauce made from ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, and sometimes plum sauce or hawthorn candy, giving the dish its characteristic bright red-orange colour and balanced tangy-sweet flavour. The dish is garnished with pineapple chunks, green bell pepper (capsicum), and onion, which add contrasting textures and a fruity-vegetal counterpoint to the rich sauce. The hallmark quality is 'wai cui nei nen' — crispy outside, tender inside. Per 100 g: ~168 kcal, 7.9 g protein, 7.1 g fat (1.7 g saturated), 17.9 g carbohydrate (7.1 g sugar), 0.6 g dietary fibre, 286 mg sodium. A standard restaurant serving (~280 g) provides approximately 470 kcal, 22 g protein, 20 g fat, 50 g carbohydrate (~20 g sugar), and ~800 mg sodium. Glycemic index ~72 (HIGH); glycemic load ~36 (VERY HIGH) — among the highest GL values of any mainstream Cantonese restaurant dish. Clinically, this dish is a significant concern for Type 2 Diabetes patients due to the combined starch batter and high-sugar sauce, and for hypertensive patients due to the sodium load from ketchup and soy-based marinade. Vitamin C (~35 mg per serving) from bell pepper and pineapple provides a modest antioxidant offset. Source: USDA SR Legacy component calculation cross-checked with HPB Singapore. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: sweet and sour pork glycemic index

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a component-level calculation using USDA SR Legacy ingredient entries cross-checked with HPB Singapore data for comparable Cantonese pork dishes. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable fried battered starch and sweet-sour sauce dishes. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Cantonese dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database; component-level fatty acid data would require full recipe disaggregation beyond the scope of this record. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this prepared composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for canola/sunflower frying oil used in standard Cantonese restaurant preparation, per USDA SR Legacy frying oil entries). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this pork and vegetable dish; pork loin contains trace vitamin D not reaching reportable threshold per USDA SR Legacy). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (50.1 g) / 100 = 36.1, rounded to 36.0. Carbohydrate per serving: 17.9 g/100g × 280 g = 50.12 g. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code for battered deep-fried Cantonese sweet-sour pork exists. FoodOn URI is null as no exact or sufficiently close FoodOn term for this prepared dish was identified as of April 2026.
